findings: Global middleware (src/middleware.ts) enforces JWT verification at the Edge for all protected routes.
Superadmin-only routes (/users, /offices, /settings/saving-measures, /physical-reports) redirect standard users to /dashboard.
All 11 API route groups have individual auth guards as defense-in-depth.
GET /api/offices now requires verifySession() — anonymous access CLOSED in Phase 6.
RBAC scoping in rbac.ts prevents non-superadmin users from accessing data outside their assigned offices.
recommendations: ADVISORY: Empty /api/generate-pdf directory exists — remove to avoid confusion.

findings: All write endpoints (POST/PUT/DELETE) require verifySuperadmin() for privileged operations.
Input validation enforced via apiSchemas.ts for /api/responses, /api/dashboard, and /api/physical-reports.
Rate limiting now covers 5 of 11 API route groups: login (5/15min), responses (60/min), dashboard (60/min), reports/bulk (5/5min), reports/summary (5/5min).
recommendations: LOW: Consider rate limiting /api/auth/change-password (5/15min) to prevent current-password brute-force.
LOW: Consider rate limiting /api/responses/classify if batch sizes can be large.

findings: Firestore Admin SDK uses parameterized query methods (.where(), .orderBy()) — not vulnerable to NoSQL injection.
All user-supplied data validated and typed via apiSchemas.ts before reaching service layers.
No raw template literal queries found in service files.
bcrypt.compare() is used for password verification — timing-safe.
Firestore-backed distributed rate limiting is correctly implemented with transactional consistency.
Heavy PDF endpoints are now rate-limited at 5 requests per 5 minutes (FIXED in Phase 6).
In-memory fallback ensures legitimate users aren't blocked if Firestore transactions fail.
Rate limit cleanup runs on a 5-minute interval in-memory.
recommendations: LOW: Firestore _system_rate_limits documents are cleaned lazily — consider a scheduled Cloud Function to purge expired docs.
ADVISORY: No WAF/CDN abuse protection layer detected at infrastructure level (outside codebase scope).

findings: Security headers implemented in next.config.ts (Phase 6): CSP, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin.
JWT_SECRET startup guard ensures secrets are never silently absent.
No secrets hardcoded in source — all via process.env.
Session cookie: httpOnly: true, sameSite: lax, secure in production.
Error stack traces gated behind NODE_ENV === 'development'.
recommendations: LOW: Consider adding Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header for production deployments.
LOW: CSRF protection relies on sameSite cookie behavior — no explicit CSRF token. Acceptable for an internal tool.

findings: AnalyticsClient.tsx uses SWR for client-side data fetching — efficient for real-time filters.
Shell.tsx sidebar uses GPU-accelerated CSS transitions.
No large assets detected in components.
recommendations: LOW: Lazy-load GraphsView and SummaryView with React.lazy() to reduce initial bundle size.

findings: Core data reads route through a CQRS read optimization layer with JSON archive fallback.
Physical reports filter by period_iso using Firestore range queries.
getAllOfficeAssignees() now uses a 5-minute memory cache (FIXED in Phase 6).
Archive route uses date_iso range queries for efficient response retrieval.
recommendations: MEDIUM: Responses collection stores dates as strings (date_iso). Migration to native Firestore Timestamps would improve query efficiency.
LOW: Verify composite Firestore index on physical_report.period_iso exists in production.

findings: JSON archive reads provide near-instant historical data loads.
Dashboard metrics API uses server-side aggregation — client receives pre-computed values.
SWR dedupingInterval: 10000 prevents redundant auth checks.
RBAC enforcement, data aggregation, and permission gating all happen server-side.
Client receives pre-computed metrics objects — not raw collections.
Analytics tab visibility enforced both server (middleware/API) and client — layered correctly.
Global middleware eliminates client-side ghost renders for unauthenticated users.
SWR deduplicates identical API requests.
Tab switching uses conditional rendering (not mounting/unmounting).
revalidateOnFocus: true keeps session state fresh.
recommendations: LOW: Consider stale-while-revalidate SWR options on office lists for faster perceived navigation.

findings: Navigation is role-aware — standard users only see applicable links.
Reports page degrades gracefully for restricted users (Data View only).
Sidebar collapses with tooltips for spatial efficiency.
Filter bars hidden/shown based on active tab context.
Industrial dark mode with consistent slate-based palette across all pages.
recommendations: LOW: Consider showing a subtle 'Contact admin for analytics access' message for restricted users.

findings: Superadmin vs standard user roles enforced at 3 layers: Edge middleware, API route guards, and service layer RBAC.
hasGlobalAccess() normalizes whitespace — prevents 'super admin' (with space) spoofing.
resolveAuthorizedOffices() is the single gateway for all office-scoped queries.
resolveAuthorizedOffices() forces non-superadmin requests through office intersection.
Both /api/responses and /api/dashboard validate and scope via resolveAuthorizedOffices() before Firestore queries.
/api/responses/classify verifies document-level office ownership for non-superadmins.
/api/reports/individual checks authorizedOffices.includes(office) before PDF generation.
Service layer queries use the resolved (not raw user-supplied) office list.
API responses are scoped — standard users never receive global datasets.
Analytics tabs hidden both at UI level and tab state forced to 'data' for non-permitted users.
SWR caches are now explicitly flushed on logout (FIXED in Phase 6) — prevents stale data bleed.
All data access validated server-side via resolveAuthorizedOffices().
No unintended global data exposure — queries enforce strict office-level scoping.
Physical reports are Superadmin-only — standard users cannot query or modify them.
recommendations: ADVISORY: Confirm Firestore security rules enforce basic auth checks as a last resort (outside codebase scope).

findings: Firestore-backed rate limiting supports horizontal scaling — shared across serverless instances.
JSON archive reads reduce Firestore load significantly for historical data.
Bulk PDF generation uses batch processing (size 5) with rate limiting (5/5min) — prevents memory exhaustion.
Assignee cache (5-min TTL) reduces redundant Firestore reads during concurrent bulk operations (FIXED in Phase 6).
recommendations: MEDIUM: Consider streaming PDF output or offloading to Cloud Tasks for very large bulk jobs.
LOW: Add server-side pagination to /api/responses if the Responses collection grows significantly.

overall.summary: The system has undergone 6 phases of security hardening and is in a strong security posture. All critical RBAC, authentication, input validation, and abuse protection concerns have been addressed. Phase 6 remediation successfully closed the remaining operational gaps. Zero critical or high-priority issues remain.
medium_priority_issues: Firestore Date string migration (Responses.date_iso) — performance optimization opportunity.
Consider streaming/queuing for very large bulk PDF jobs.
low_priority_issues: Rate limit /api/auth/change-password to prevent current-password brute-force.
Lazy-load GraphsView and SummaryView for bundle optimization.
Rate limit Firestore document TTL cleanup via scheduled function.
